message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 9 (36 todo)
Recent changes: Green added a hardcoded isBlockOfThree check (exactly 3 same-type items → base 60) alongside the existing per-item sum logic.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to next test after completion. Keep all tests green. Attempt at least one refactoring or document why none is warranted. Note: upcoming tests (4 runes no-block, 7 runes one block + leftover, mixed types, two blocks) will generalize the block logic, so avoid premature generalization but improve clarity where warranted.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
All 9 tests pass. Now let me work through the refactoring process.

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)

- `runScenario`: Clearly reveals intent — runs a scenario, returns results. Keep.
- `isBlockOfThree`: Describes a "block of three same-type items." Accurate for current behavior. Keep.
- `basePremium`, `basePremiums`, `PROCESSING_FEE`: All clear and domain-aligned. Keep.

Naming is solid. No rename warranted.

## Step 2: Calculate Initial APP Mass

Focusing on the `runScenario` function body (the code Green just touched):

```
const results = scenario.steps.map((step) => {
  const isBlockOfThree =
    step.items.length === 3 &&
    step.items.every((item) => item.type === step.items[0].type);
  const basePremium = isBlockOfThree
    ? 60
    : step.items.reduce((sum, item) => sum + basePremiums[item.type], 0);
  return { premium: basePremium + PROCESSING_FEE };
});
return { results };
```

Component count:
- Constants: `3`, `60`, `0` = 3 → 3
- Bindings: `results`, `step`, `isBlockOfThree`, `item`(every), `basePremium`, `sum`, `item`(reduce) = 7 → 7
- Invocations: `map`, `.length`(property, not counted), `.every`, `===`, `&&`(operator), `.reduce`, `+`(basePremiums sum), `basePremiums[]` index, `+`(fee) — counting function/operator invocations: `map`, `every`, `reduce`, `===`, `+`, `+` = ~6 → 12
- Conditionals: `?:` ternary = 1 → 4
- Loops: `map`, `every`, `reduce` = 3 → 15
- Assignments: 0 → 0

Total Mass ≈ 41 (approximate; the exact figure matters less than the comparison).

## Step 3: Apply Simple Design Rules

**Rule 1 (Tests Pass):** All 9 green.

**Rule 2 (Reveals Intent):** This is where the win is. The `step.items[0].type` repeated reference and the inline ternary with magic literal `60` partly obscure intent. The hardcoded `60` is a known stand-in (per the prompt, block logic is intentionally not yet generalized). I should not generalize it (premature — upcoming tests will drive that). But I can improve clarity by extracting the block-detection predicate into a named helper, making `runScenario` read as a clear sequence: detect block, compute base, add fee.

**Rule 3 (No Duplication):** `step.items[0]` appears once; no real duplication yet.

**Rule 4 (Fewest Elements):** Don't add elements without payoff. Extracting one well-named helper improves clarity (Rule 2) and isolates the soon-to-change block logic — a worthwhile structural improvement that does not generalize prematurely.

## Step 4: Implement Refactoring

Extracting `isBlockOfThree` as a named helper improves clarity and localizes the hardcoded logic that upcoming tests will replace.

content.text:
All 9 tests still green.

## Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass

The `runScenario` body is now much lighter:

```
const results = scenario.steps.map((step) => {
  const basePremium = isBlockOfThree(step.items)
    ? BLOCK_OF_THREE_BASE
    : sumOfBasePremiums(step.items);
  return { premium: basePremium + PROCESSING_FEE };
});
return { results };
```

- Constants: 0 (literals moved to named bindings) → 0
- Bindings: `results`, `step`, `basePremium` = 3 → 3
- Invocations: `map`, `isBlockOfThree`, `sumOfBasePremiums`, `+` = 4 → 8
- Conditionals: `?:` = 1 → 4
- Loops: `map` = 1 → 5
- Assignments: 0 → 0

`runScenario` body mass ≈ 20 (down from ~41). The loop/conditional complexity of block-detection and summation now lives in two small, single-purpose, well-named functions. Total mass across the module is roughly the same (logic was relocated, not removed), but the orchestration function is dramatically clearer and each helper has minimal local mass.

## Step 6: Document Decision

**Refactoring Applied:**
- Rule 2 (Reveals Intent): Extracted `isBlockOfThree(items)` and `sumOfBasePremiums(items)` as named helpers. `runScenario` now reads as a clear sentence: "base premium is the block rate when it's a block of three, otherwise the sum of base premiums; add the processing fee."
- Rule 2: Replaced the magic literal `60` with the named constant `BLOCK_OF_THREE_BASE`, consistent with the existing `PROCESSING_FEE` style.
- Rule 4 (Fewest Elements): Added two helpers and one constant — each earns its place by clarifying intent and isolating the block logic that upcoming tests (4 runes, 7 runes, mixed types, two blocks) will generalize.

**Deliberately NOT done (avoiding premature generalization):**
- Did not generalize the hardcoded `BLOCK_OF_THREE_BASE = 60` into "compute discount per block." Per guidance, the upcoming tests will drive that generalization. The current extraction localizes that change to one tiny function (`isBlockOfThree` + the constant), so future generalization will be low-friction.

**Naming:** Evaluated first; existing names were already accurate, so no renames. New extractions named to reveal intent.

## Step 7: Report Completion

🔄 Refactor Phase Complete:
- **Refactoring:** Extracted `isBlockOfThree(items)` and `sumOfBasePremiums(items)` helpers; introduced `BLOCK_OF_THREE_BASE` named constant to replace the magic `60`.
- **Mass Change:** `runScenario` body ~41 → ~20 (complexity relocated into two minimal, named functions; orchestration now clearly readable).
- **Tests:** All 9 passing ✅ (36 todo remain).

Implementation file: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-29_07-22-49_claim-office-example-mapping_v4-exact-subagents_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts`

The block logic remains intentionally hardcoded (not prematurely generalized) but is now isolated in a single small helper, ready for the upcoming 4-runes / 7-runes / mixed-types / two-blocks tests to drive generalization. Proceeding to the next test (Red phase for "should charge 100 G base for 4 runes").
